params.text:
Why we report accidents and incidents

Every day in adult social care, things happen that we did not plan for. Someone slips on a wet floor, a medicine is given at the wrong time, or a piece of equipment fails. When we report these events honestly and quickly, we protect the people we support, our colleagues and ourselves.

Reporting is not about pointing the finger. It exists for three linked reasons:

  • Safety – so risks can be reduced before someone is harmed.
  • Learning – so the whole service improves and the same thing does not happen again.
  • Legal duty – because the law and our regulators require it.

This short course explains what to report, how to record it factually, and the legal duties that sit behind it — including RIDDOR, the Health and Safety at Work Act 1974, and the Care Quality Commission (CQC).

The law behind reporting

Several rules shape how we report in care settings:

  • The Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 places a general duty on employers to protect the health, safety and welfare of workers and others affected by the work.
  • RIDDOR 2013 (the Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations) requires certain serious events to be reported to the Health and Safety Executive (HSE).
  • The CQC requires providers to send statutory notifications about certain events and to follow the duty of candour when something goes wrong.

You do not need to be a legal expert. Your job is to recognise events, record them accurately, and pass them to the right person promptly.
